issue_title: Assertion 'GDK_IS_SCREEN (screen)' fails in frozen app
issue_body:
Firstly, thanks for sharing this library. The concept is very elegant and useful.

I want to look up the icon for a directory (`/home/michael` in the code below):

```
import pgi
pgi.install_as_gi()
import gi
gi.require_version('Gtk', '3.0')
from gi.repository import Gtk,Gio

file_path = '/home/michael'

f = Gio.file_new_for_path(file_path)
file_info = f.query_info('standard::icon', 0, None)
icon_name = file_info.get_icon().get_names()[0]
icon_theme = Gtk.IconTheme.get_default()
icon_filename = icon_theme.lookup_icon(icon_name, 32, 0)
print(icon_filename.get_filename())
```

This works fine when I do it in my Python shell. However, when I freeze the above code with [PyInstaller](http://www.pyinstaller.org/), I get:

```
(process:15580): Gtk-CRITICAL **: gtk_icon_theme_get_for_screen: assertion 'GDK_IS_SCREEN (screen)' failed
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "main.py", line 13, in <module>
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'lookup_icon'
Failed to execute script main
```

The main problem seems to be the failing assertion `GDK_IS_SCREEN`. Googling for this assertion, I found that it may be related to the environment variables `DISPLAY` and `XDG_CURRENT_DESKTOP`. Unfortunately, that doesn't seem to be it as they are both set correctly (to `:0` and `Unity`, respectively).

Do you happen to know what else could cause the assertion `GDK_IS_SCREEN` to fail, in a frozen app?

issue_title: I think duplex I/O on SSL sockets can break the kernel's invariants?
issue_body:
Suppose I have two tasks, one of which is looping on reading from an SSL socket and one of which is looping on writing to that same socket. (This would happen, e.g., in an HTTP/2 implementation.) In general, this kind of socket usage is totally OK and explicitly supported by curio. But, curio does have a rule that you can't have two tasks that are both trying to read from the same socket at the same time, or both trying to write to the same socket at the same time.

...But with an SSL socket, doing `recv` can trigger a `_write_wait`, and `send` can trigger a `_read_wait`, which means that if one task is calling `recv` and another task is calling `send`, then they might both end up in `_read_wait` or `_write_wait` at the same time, and then the kernel will get pissed off.

This is unfortunately difficult to test for too, since it's very rare that an SSL socket *actually* needs to read before it can write or vice-versa, but it does happen.

issue_title: Making timeout_after/ignore_after truly composable
issue_body:
[As you know](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/46#issuecomment-216083910) I'm a big fan on `timeout_after`. The awesome thing about it is that it's composable, in the sense that I can treat any random coroutine as a black box and slap a `timeout_after` around it to impose a timeout on it, so long as the called coroutine follows some simple rules. Basically, it should be written so that my timeout fires, then it promptly cleans up and exits. Not an onerous constraint.

Except... it's hard to follow this rule in coroutines that themselves want to use `timeout_after`. The docs allude to some of this; here's an example where everything goes wrong because the internal coroutine can't tell whether it's the internal timeout that fired, or the outer one:

```python
  async def get_first_responding(urls):
      for url in urls:
          try:
              # per-URL timeout
              async with timeout_after(5):
                  return (await curio_http.get(url)).text
          except TaskTimeout:
              print("Too slow! Trying the next")
      raise RuntimeError("everyone's too slow!")

  async def main():
      # Global timeout:
      async with timeout_after(10):
          print(await get_first_responding([...]))
```

I suggest that the key missing feature is that when we see a timeout exception, we need to be able to whether it means that *we* have timed out and should clean up and let our caller get on with things, versus one that means *a thing we called* has timed out and we should recover however makes sense (e.g. by falling back to trying another server). Are we inside the `timeout_after` that fired, or outside?

```
 outer code       <--- Are we here?
   timeout_after
     inner code       <-- or here?
```

Proposal: arrange for the inner code to see a different exception type than the outer code.

Implementation: give each `timeout_after` a magic cookie, and when a timeout fires, inject an instance of the "inner" exception type with the magic cookie attached. When an exception passes through a `timeout_after`, check if the cookies match, and if so, convert to the "outer" exception type. (And `ignore_after` should check the cookie, and swallow the exception iff it matches.)

When there are stacked timeouts that fire simultaneously, e.g.

#+BEGIN_SRC python
  async with timeout_after(5):
      # -- mark --
      async with timeout_after(10):
          ...
#+END_SRC

then it should always be the outermost timeout whose cookie is used (so the line at `-- mark --` should see an "inner" exception, not an "outer" one).

Unsolved problem: I am drawing a blank on what to call these two exception types to distinguish them :-(. OperationTimeOut versus CancelledTimeOut?

(Speaking of timeout exception names, why is it called `TaskTimeout`? It doesn't really have anything to do with Tasks in particular, does it? I mean, any more so than literally everything else in curio?)

issue_title: unit prefix does not respect case_sensitive setting on parse_expression
issue_body:
when using `parse_expression` with `case_sensitive=False` the prefix case is not ignored. For example the following case will work (units is a unitRegistry): 
```python
x = units.parse_expression("kiloGrams", case_sensitive=False)
```
but the following will raise an exception:
```python
x = units.parse_expression("Kilograms", case_sensitive=False)
```
here is the exception:
```
pint.errors.UndefinedUnitError: 'Kilograms' is not defined in the unit registry
```

issue_title: Using sudo pip install is a bad idea
issue_body:
Using `sudo pip install ...` is a bad idea as managing the packages of the system python is the job of system's package manager. it should instead be `pip install --user ...`.

This would be a change for both the README and command substitution behaviour.

See pypa/pip#1668

issue_title: Global switch for type checking mode
issue_body:
All the types are currently checked invariantly (i.e. it has to be the same as specified or it fails otherwise). This sometimes might not what our users expect. I think we should implement a global switch to change the type checking mode.

This switch should be able to specify whether type checking is: invariant, covariant, contravariant or bivariant.

issue_title: sniff_continuously has a backlog of packets and doesn't generate them immediately
issue_body:
I see this both in Python 2.7 and 3.4. 

In this commit: https://github.com/goretkin/pyshark/commit/9ef4ab5cbeb92f16b64f37ad754e09bbf6b369b6 I've added a simple test to illustrate the issue and see if anyone else experiences it.

In my case, I am debugging a USB protocol, which generates pairs of "packets" at once. pyshark isn't dropping any packets, but the sniff_continuously generator appears to yield only once per burst of "packets", even if there's more than one.

Here's how I trigger the behavior. `tshark_stub` emulates `tshark` and emits two "packets" in quick succession when it receives `SIGUSR1`. `test_continuous_capture.py` sets the tshark path to be this `tshark_stub` and then prints a line each time `sniff_continously` yields a new "packet".

So in Terminal 1, I run `test_continuous_capture.py`
```bash
pyshark/tests/capture$ python test_continuous_capture.py 
[2015-02-12 09:32:24.362951] DEBUG: LiveCapture: Creating TShark subprocess with parameters: ./tshark_stub -n -T pdml -i 1
[2015-02-12 09:32:24.365716] DEBUG: LiveCapture: TShark subprocess created
```

On Terminal 2, I do `ps aux | grep tshark_stub` to find the PID of the stub, and with an eye on Terminal 1 I do a couple of these
```
~$ kill -SIGUSR1 8018
~$ kill -SIGUSR1 8018
```

I expect four lines to be printed in Terminal 1, but instead I only see 
```
[2015-02-12 09:32:24.365716] DEBUG: LiveCapture: TShark subprocess created
sniffed packet
sniffed packet
```

Finally, in Terminal 2, I run `kill 8018`. Terminal 1 shows
```
[2015-02-12 09:32:24.365716] DEBUG: LiveCapture: TShark subprocess created
sniffed packet
sniffed packet
sniffed packet
sniffed packet
[2015-02-12 09:37:52.599719] DEBUG: LiveCapture: EOF reached (sync)
```

A similar thing happens if `tshark_stub` emits only one packet at a time. The first packet doesn't cause a yield, but every packet thereafter does. But it's forever offset by 1, the packet just yielded and the actual packet sniffed, until EOF.

issue_title: Windows: provide APIs to deal with Windows services
issue_body:
Rationale
=======

People who want to deal with Windows services in Python are forced to rely either on pywin32 or WMI extensions but they're too low level (pywin32) and a bit confusing IMO. Some examples:

- list(): http://www.thecodingforums.com/threads/listing-existing-windows-services-with-python.715123/
- create():  http://code.activestate.com/recipes/576451-how-to-create-a-windows-service-in-python/
- remove(): https://bytes.com/topic/python/answers/32671-win32serviceutil-remove-service
- modify(): http://code.activestate.com/recipes/59872-manipulating-windows-services/

It would be nice if psutil could provide a compact API to deal with Windows services more easily.

API
===

I'm still not sure how to organize the API (classes? functions? namedtuple? etc.) yet, but roughly the underlying C extension module should expose these APIs:

- `list()`
- `create(name, exe_path, start_type='auto', description=None, ...)`
- `remove(name)`
- `modify(name, description=None, start_type=None, ...)`
- `start(name)`
- `stop(name)`
- `restart(name)`

psutil might wrap them by providing 3 higher-level functions + one class:

- `win_service_list()` -> return a list or `WindowsService` classes
- `win_service_install(name, exe_path, start_type='auto', description=None, ...)`
- `win_service_get(name)` -> return a `WindowsService` instance with the following attrs/methods:
      - `name`
      - `description`
      - `start_type`
      - `status`
      - `start()`
      - `stop()`
      - `restart()`
      - `modify()`
      - `remove()`

I think it's a good idea to stay as minimal as possible, meaning we should not provide all the features offered by pywin32. Instead we should cover the most common use cases only, which are list/create/modify/remove and start/stop/restart.

References
=========

- remove() and create(): http://web.mit.edu/source-8.1/third/xntp/scripts/wininstall/instsrv/instsrv.c
- pywin32 service utils: http://pyxr.sourceforge.net/PyXR/c/python24/lib/site-packages/win32/lib/win32serviceutil.py.html

issue_title: KafkaConsumer always passes message key to deserializer, even if key is None
issue_body:
I specified `json.loads` as the `KafkaConsumer`'s `key_deserializer` callable, then produced a message without a key.

`json.loads` throws an exception about expecting a string. Looks like root cause is here: https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/master/kafka/consumer/fetcher.py#L546

Fetcher is always using passing the key to the deserializer, even if the key is `None`.

Will submit a PR shortly.

issue_title: Some FS and LAN metrics fail to export correctly to StatsD
issue_body:
#### Description
When running with --export-statsd; some metrics are not translating over correctly and throw exceptions in StatsD.

- FS metrics are sent with special characters in the stats name (On windows, so filesystem = C:\\, which then fails in StatsD) 
-- 27 Mar 11:01:29 - DEBUG: Bad line: **\\.free** in msg "glances.fs.**C:\\**.free:3366996787
- LAN Metrics send boolean True/False instead of numeric, and get a similar error.
--27 Mar 11:01:29 - DEBUG: Bad line: **False**,g in msg "glances.network.Local Area Connection* 11.is_up:False|g"
--27 Mar 11:01:29 - DEBUG: Bad line: **False**,g in msg "glances.network.isatap.mtg.home.ads.is_up:False|g"
--27 Mar 11:01:29 - DEBUG: Bad line: **True**,g in msg "glances.network.Local Area Connection.is_up:True|g"
--27 Mar 11:01:29 - DEBUG: Bad line: **True**,g in msg "glances.network.Loopback Pseudo-Interface 1.is_up:True|g"

A naive solution might be to filter out non-alphanum in the stat_name:
`stat_name = filter(lambda x: str.isalnum(x) or x == '.',stat_name)`
I don't know if that would play nice across all platforms though, maybe it would make more sense to swap \ and / to _.  
Then convert the stat_value to int when sending the gauge, so it's within the try/catch just in case this fails.  The bools translate nicely over to 0/1.  I don't think you'd ever want a non-int metric value here
`try:`
`    self.client.gauge(stat_name, int(stat_value))`
`except Exception as e:`
`    logger.error("Can not export stats to Statsd (%s)" % e)`


#### Versions

* Glances (glances -V): v2.8.8
* PSutil (glances -V): v5.2.1
* Operating System (lsb_release -a): Windows 7

Below is the StatsD Logfile, does not appear in Glances log (which makes sense - metrics are delivered)

issue_title: Entity recognition should process sentence words
issue_body:
Hi,

Just found out that [`entities` function](https://github.com/aboSamoor/polyglot/blob/master/polyglot/text.py#L118) process `BaseBlob` words list where in some cases it produce false positives by an merging entity at the end of a sentence and another entity at the start of next sentence.

Here's the example,

```python
In [1]: from polyglot.text import Text, Word

In [2]: blob = u"""Momentum perbaikan "Los Blaugranas" itu awalnya justru terjadi setelah Martin Caceres diusir wasit karena melakukan pelanggaran keras di kotak penalti. Meski bermain dengan sepuluh pemain, rasa percaya diri mereka bangkit setelah kiper Jose Manuel Pinto berhasil menggagalkan tendangan penalti Jose Luis Marti.

Barca semakin kuat setelah Messi masuk pada menit ke-58. Messi pula yang mencetak gol penyama skor pada menit ke-80."""

In [3]: text = Text(blob)

In [4]: text.entities
Out[4]:
[I-PER([u'Martin', u'Caceres']),
 I-PER([u'Jose', u'Manuel', u'Pinto']),
 I-PER([u'Jose', u'Luis', u'Marti', u'.', u'Barca']),
 I-PER([u'Messi']),
 I-PER([u'Messi'])]
```

Since there's `SentenceTokenizer` function, I think it would be best to process the `Sentence` words rather than the `BaseBlob` words. I know it'll require extra step to create sentence objects, but the result will be better.

```diff
--- text.py.orig        2015-05-11 16:25:54.249957999 +0000
+++ .../lib/python2.7/site-packages/polyglot/text.py    2015-05-11 16:26:53.001957999 +0000
@@ -117,21 +117,22 @@
   @cached_property
   def entities(self):
     """Returns a list of entities for this blob."""
```

issue_title: [discussion] Dynamic pin code and setup ids, and persistence of data.
issue_body:
Reading through the Apple HAP documentation, it's pretty clear that "if a device _can_ dynamically generate pin codes, it _should_."

To that end, I started playing around with stuff, and a dynamically generated pin code has no drawbacks (other than having to see what value was generated this time around).

I believe the same is true of setup id (which is used for the URL generation in https://github.com/ikalchev/HAP-python/pull/31 and https://github.com/ikalchev/HAP-python/pull/30). To that end, I believe that these values should _not_ be stored in the pickle.

The other thing I discovered while developing accessories is that pickle is not a great way of storing stuff, especially during development. It means that if you change a class, or an import, then things can stop working, and you'd need to delete the pickle file and then re-add the accessory. There are also possible security implications of pickling data, but I'm less concerned about that because we aren't unpickling user supplied data.

issue_body:
For example:

```python
x = y.\
    foo(
        bar,
    )
```

The current tokenization is:

```
1:0 NAME 'x'
?:? UNIMPORTANT_WS ' '
1:2 OP '='
?:? UNIMPORTANT_WS ' '
1:4 NAME 'y'
1:5 OP '.'
?:? UNIMPORTANT_WS '\\\n    '
2:4 NAME 'foo'
2:7 OP '('
2:8 NL '\n'
?:? UNIMPORTANT_WS '        '
3:8 NAME 'bar'
3:11 OP ','
3:12 NL '\n'
?:? UNIMPORTANT_WS '    '
4:4 OP ')'
4:5 NEWLINE '\n'
5:0 ENDMARKER ''
```

It would be cool to split the UNIMPORTANT_WS token which contains the escaped newline

issue_title: local multicast/MDNS for relay
issue_body:
A friend and I were able to transfer a file directly, when both of us were normally using a VPN (which means the data has to go through the relay, very slow), by switching to a local ad-hoc network. (on OS-X, this is done by choosing "Create Network.." from the wifi menu). To accomplish this, I had to run a relay locally, and he had to use `--relay-url=ws://MY-IP-ADDR:4000/v1`. Neither of us were connected to the real internet while on the ad-hoc network, so the normal relay wasn't available.

It'd be slick to have a mode that does this without running a local relay. MCT and I were thinking about this a while ago. I'm imagining something like this:

* use `--relay-url=multicast`, or `--relay-multicast`, or MDNS, or some flag to say "use the local network instead of the default relay server"
* replace the rendezvous protocol client with one that joins a multicast group, sends periodic messages to the group until the other client responds, then sends messages directly to the other client

There might be a cheaper/easier/less-coding way to go, like by having a distinguished side (maybe the sender) run a full rendezvous server (with an in-memory database instead of `relay.sqlite` on disk), then register it on a well-known MDNS/zeroconf/bonjour name. The receiver could repeatedly try to connect to a relay at that well-known name, and then speak the normal relay protocol.

issue_title: `FreshdeskModel._keys` set is shared between different submodels
issue_body:
In the code there is a base class `FreshdeskModel` and submodels like `Ticket` or `Group` etc. In the base class the keys from JSON are stored into `_keys` set, but the problem is that this set is shared on base class level, i.e. singleton. So whenever you work with multiple models and access the `_keys` collection it will contain all keys from all models.

Steps to reproduce (example):
1) Query for ticket(s)
2) Query for contact(s)
3) Access `_keys` collection from any of the models
=> it contains keys from both tickets and contact

The `test_python_tpl_with_table_no_lang` test ([Source here](https://github.com/tlatsas/jinja2-highlight/blob/b8a632833464dfecefda5c837b6f35bc1cc79a15/tests/test_highlight.py#L136-L144)) is failing because Pygment's `guess_lexer` doesn't seem to catch extremely basic Python.

```python
pygments.util.ClassNotFound: no lexer matching the text found
```

`test_python_tpl_with_inline_no_lang` is naturally failing as well.

issue_title: watcher.py generates many local-wallet screens when wallet is large.
issue_body:
more of an info/wishlist than a bug.

Large wallets take longer than one second to load. The python script will keep trying until the wallet is loaded, sometimes spawning multiple screens. That's why it's a good idea to only have the needed keys, and cut away the cruft.

$ screen -ls
There are screens on:
	15269.local-wallet	(12/14/2015 10:22:40 AM)	(Detached)
	15263.local-wallet	(12/14/2015 10:22:29 AM)	(Detached)
	15254.local-wallet	(12/14/2015 10:22:18 AM)	(Detached)
	15248.local-wallet	(12/14/2015 10:22:07 AM)	(Detached)
	14699.witness	(12/14/2015 10:05:34 AM)	(Detached)
	14642.pts-0.bitshares2	(12/14/2015 10:04:18 AM)	(Detached)

each local-wallet has a running cli_wallet. That causes problems with the pricefeed script as each cli_wallet is listening on the same port.

```
    while result == None:
        try:
            print("waiting ...")
            subprocess.call(["screen","-dmS","local-wallet",config.path_to_cli_wallet,"-H",config.rpc_port,"-w",config.path_to_wallet_json])
            time.sleep(1)
            result = rpc.info()
        except:
            time.sleep(10)
            pass
```

Either increase the timeout  time.sleep(1), or only use watcher.py with smaller wallets.

issue_title: Performance Issue
issue_body:
As the readme doesn't really state it: django-rest-knox has a major performance issue.

https://github.com/James1345/django-rest-knox/blob/master/knox/auth.py#L50

This line leads to _major_ performance hits once one reaches significant user numbers, as each API request leads to all tokens being sent do the Django instances. Once reaching a few tens of thousands of users and more than a handful of requests per second, the application is spending more time receiving/parsing SQL and guessing tokens than doing some actual work.

One way to change this would be the following: Split the token in 2 parts:
- Part one is an identifier for a database column,  e.g.  a random string
- Part two is the very token knox currently uses

This would lead to only querying for a single row (the one matching part on), then comparing one hash.

Funfact: While loadtesting I watched my servers handle > 100mbit/s of SQL due to this bug.

issue_title: Server failure unhandled
issue_body:
If a server and client make a connection, and then the server restarts, the client fails to successfully reconnect.

On the lower level, the client believes that it has connected again and receives heartbeats from the server, but the server never receives a connection request. On the client side, I have no notification that anything is wrong. No disconnect callback occurs since the connection was forcibly reset. This means the client sits in recv() waiting for data that the server doesn't know to give it.

To demonstrate this problem: start a server and client with a valid connection, kill the server, restart the server. The client will fail to successfully reconnect.

Fixing reconnections to the server would be useful. Alternatively, if the user was given some type of exception or callback that a disconnect had occurred, they could deal with it themselves. Trying automatically and silently to reconnect to the server and failing is a problem.

issue_body:
According to the documentation it would seem that the following

```
class Namespace(BaseNamespace):
    def on_connect(self):
        print('[Connected]')
    ...

socketIO = SocketIO('localhost', 8000, Namespace)
socketIO.wait(seconds=1)
```
ought to fire the `on_connect` callback for the root namespace, however I'm not seeing this behavior.  I see the following outputted:
```
dwalker@host:/src › python client.py
DEBUG:socketIO-client:127.0.0.1:8000/socket.io [engine.io transport selected] websocket
DEBUG:socketIO-client:127.0.0.1:8000/socket.io [engine.io heartbeat reset]
```

However, if I change the code to use the .define() we do see the on_connect called for a given new namespace

```

```
we get the following

issue_title: Race condition causes file descriptors leak
issue_body:
I have a simple 1:1 chat application.
It is built with these components:

Flask==0.12.1
Flask-SocketIO==2.9.0
python-engineio==1.6.1
python-socketio==1.7.5

It uses async_mode='eventlet'.

The chat clients connect to the server and exchange messages. If a chat client is disconnected then the server sends a message to the other client in the room.

The file descriptors leak happens in the following case.
- Both chat clients are joined in a room.
- Both chat clients are created in single process. This process is a test application.
- The test application process is killed.
- The server receives events 'disconnect' for both clients.

The 'disconnect' event handle code is something like this:

@socketio.on('disconnect')
def client_disconnect():
  emit('sig-user-leaved', {'client_id': client_id}, room=room, include_self=False)

I traced the problem in the following code: socket.py end of the method _websocket_handler.
----
        self.queue.put(None)  # unlock the writer task so that it can exit
        writer_task.join()
        self.close(wait=True, abort=True)
----

In my case the following happens:
-------------
client1: self.queue.put(None)
client1: writer_task.join()
client2: self.queue.put(None)
client2: writer_task.join()
client1: self.close(wait=True, abort=True)
             self.server._trigger_event('disconnect')
                    emit('sig-user-leaved', {'client_id': client_id}, room=room, include_self=False)
                         client2: send() { self.queue.put(pkt) } # !!!!!!!! THIS CAUSES THE PROBLEM
client2: self.close(wait=True, abort=True)                                        
-------------

There should be a guard that after 
self.queue.put(None)
no new data is added in the queue before close() is called.

```
@api_view(['POST'])
```

issue_title: Feature request for In-Place Activated BatchNorm
issue_body:
In-Place Activated BatchNorm (InPlace-ABN) is a novel approach to reduce the memory required for training deep networks. It allows for up to 50% memory savings in modern architectures such as ResNet, ResNeXt and Wider ResNet by redefining BN + non linear activation as a single in-place operation, while smartly dropping or recomputing intermediate buffers as needed.

https://github.com/mapillary/inplace_abn
https://arxiv.org/pdf/1712.02616.pdf

issue_title: MakeLoss should accept optional output symbol
issue_body:
I would like to suggest that the `MakeLoss` symbol constructor takes an optional output symbol. When it is provided, the forward output of MakeLoss would be the same as the provided output symbol, while the backward operation would be using the gradient of the input loss.

The value of this approach is that it means you can more easily retrieve the prediction values when using an executor bound to a custom loss without a second executor for the earlier prediction symbol. In particular, `Module`'s `predict` method would then correctly output the prediction, not the error, when built around a custom MakeLoss loss symbol.

Example usage for "custom" squared error on liner regression:
```
x = mx.sym.Variable('data')
y = mx.sym.FullyConnected(data=data, num_hidden=1)
label = mx.sym.Variable('label')
loss = mx.sym.MakeLoss(mx.sym.square(y - label), output=y)

ex = loss.simple_bind(mx.cpu(), data=(32, 2))
# assign inputs and label here...
prediction = ex.forward(is_train=True) # returns predicted values instead of loss!
ex.backward()
# now doing training update step using gradients in ex; e.g, with an optimizer...
```

issue_title: Automatic context to select CPU or GPU(s)
issue_body:
This is a feature request.

It would be very convenient to have MXNet automatically detect if a GPU is available and use it.  Or if there is more than one available, use all GPUs.  This would avoid a lot of wasted boiler-plate code where people have to specify the context manually, which is usually hard-coded or sometimes passed in from the command line.

Certainly there are times when you don't want to use all the available hardware.  Either if you're sharing hardware with others, or in those cases where MXNet doesn't yet scale out well.  But it's super common to just want to use whatever the machine has -- especially in cloud computing environments.

## Description

1. Run MXNet on mac with 2 cpu cores. Monitor cpu usage percentage. It should be close to 200%.

issue_title: Build timeouts after ccache added
issue_body:
Some rare build failures occur after the addition of ccache. Sample: 

http://jenkins.mxnet-ci.amazon-ml.com/blue/rest/organizations/jenkins/pipelines/incubator-mxnet/branches/PR-11486/runs/1/nodes/57/log/?start=0

http://jenkins.mxnet-ci.amazon-ml.com/blue/organizations/jenkins/incubator-mxnet/detail/PR-11486/1/pipeline/

The builds are hitting the Jenkin's timeout as the task fails at exactly the 2h mark.

The machine will probably have a running container until it's down-scaled due to the zombie container bug.

Adding build retries and lowering the Jenkins build timeout should improve the situation.

issue_title: Add an --audit functionality to audit created baseline
issue_body:
Currently, we use the baseline for two purposes:

1. Acknowledgement of current true positive secrets in the code base, AND
2. Whitelisted false positives.

For migratory purposes, we need some way to distinguish them, so that we can aggregate how many secrets left to move to a more secure storage. Therefore, the proposed way of displaying secrets in the baseline is:

```
{
    "type": "High Entropy String",
    "line_number": 19,
    "hashed_secret": "b8b693a3759e023b509093f4cacf0a3c973266fc",
    "is_secret": false,
}
```

Then, we should make an `--audit` command line flag that prompts users through each of their secrets found, verifies with them whether it is indeed a secret, and makes the appropriate baseline change.

issue_title: Increase threadsafety level in impyla
Closing socket causes poll to error with asyncio
issue_body:
In my application, I want to close the socket cleanly using `socket.close()`:

```
import zmq.asyncio
import zmq
import asyncio

context = zmq.asyncio.Context()
socket = context.socket(zmq.ROUTER)
socket.bind_to_random_port('tcp://127.0.0.1')
msg = await socket.recv_multipart()

# Some time later...
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
for task in asyncio.Task.all_tasks():
    loop.call_soon_threadsafe(task.cancel)
socket.close()
context.destroy()
```

issue_title: Precomputing the ruptures
issue_body:
Here is strategy that might be viable for large event based calculations (i.e. > 65,536 sites).

1. precompute the ruptures for the full source model, with no prefiltering
2. split the exposure in spatial tiles with less than 65,536 sites each and run multiple event based risk
    calculations starting from the precomputed ruptures
3. collect together the results coming from the different calculations

This is hard to implement in practice, since it is not clear how to manage the logic trees (different tiles will have different logic trees). We could restrict ourselves to collect only the statistical results. Or, like in UCERF, we could be interested only in the total portfolio losses per each realization.

issue_title: AssumeRoleProvider shadows SharedCredentialProvider
issue_body:
In the default provider chain, `AssumeRoleProvider` has precedence over `SharedCredentialProvider`. The result is that valid session credentials in `~/.aws/credentials` are ignored when the profile has a `role_arn` value.

We write temporary session credentials for several profiles to `~/.aws/credentials` (not via botocore). These profiles are using assume-role, so to adhere to the standard setup and play nice with other tools, we like to also set `role_arn` for them in `~/.aws/config`. Unfortunately, botocore doesn't use the written credentials in that case.

issue_title: s3 config with use_accelerate_endpoint and generate_presigned_post does not work
issue_body:
This code:

```
    s3 = boto3.client('s3', config=Config(s3={'use_accelerate_endpoint': True}))
    s3.generate_presigned_post(
        Bucket = 'some bucket,
        Key = 'some key'
    )
```

...will not result in an accelerated URL being generated for a presigned post operation.  The bug is in this code, in `client.py`:

```
        # Enable accelerate if the configuration is set to to true or the
        # endpoint being used matches one of the Accelerate endpoints.
        if s3_accelerate or S3_ACCELERATE_ENDPOINT in self._endpoint.host:
            # Amazon S3 accelerate is being used then always use the virtual
            # style of addressing because it is required.
            self._force_virtual_style_s3_addressing()
            # Also make sure that the hostname gets switched to
            # s3-accelerate.amazonaws.com
            self.meta.events.register_first(
                'request-created.s3', switch_host_s3_accelerate)
```

...`request-created.s3` is never called for a presigned post because no request to s3 is made, and thus `switch_host_s3_accelerate` is not called.

issue_title: Invalid datetime can produce partial parse. Should return None instead
issue_body:
I came across this silent failure when parsing a third party datetime with an unusual separator:

```python
>>> import ciso8601

>>> ciso8601.parse_datetime('2017-05-29 17:36:00Z')
datetime.datetime(2017, 5, 29, 17, 36, tzinfo=<UTC>)

#Now for a completely invalid string
>>> print(ciso8601.parse_datetime('Completely invalid!'))
None

#Now for an out of spec string (invalid separator)
>>> ciso8601.parse_datetime('2017-05-29_17:36:00Z')
datetime.datetime(2017, 5, 29, 0, 0)
```

It seems that the parser sees a character that it doesn't recognize and stops parsing?

I would have expected that if the entire string cannot be parsed, it would return `None` as `parse_datetime` does for other parse failures, not silently return a datetime without time or `tzinfo`. 

This lead to a sneaky bug in the code.

issue_title: Creating an expectation with times() implies with_args()
issue_body:
When creating an expectation using .exactly(n).times() (with the explicit function invocation), it implies `with_args()`.

So, this expectation:

```
(expect(PrestoRepository)._more_results_available
    .exactly(3).times()
    .and_return(True, True, False))
```

will fail if `_more_results_available` is called with any arguments.

This seems pretty unintuitive to me - I understand `times` has a property decorator, but the method ought to be a no-op instead of implicitly calling `with_args`, or it creates some confusing expectation violations.

issue_title: A race condition results in get_results never returning

```
            item_to_ventilate = self._items_to_ventilate[self._current_item_to_ventilate]
```

issue_title: Some packages are not installed (Debian, Raspberry Pi)
issue_body:
I'm trying this out on a Raspberry Pi running Raspbian (in effect, Debian) Jessie in a Python 3.4 virtualenv.

After having apparently installed dependencies, and during the ``Creating the project`` phase, I'll get tracebacks.

First it was an import error for polymorphic. ``pip freeze`` confirmed it wasn't installed. ``pip install django-filer`` *did* install it, and I was able to continue.

Then, an import error for appconf, also not installed. And so on.

It appears that something in the chain of requirements is breaking, without it being picked up at all. Not sure whether this is an issue in Raspbian, 3.4 or the installer.

issue_body:
Hi, this is a question - not an issue.

I have some older code that use the following import:

    from setuptools.compat import reraise

which now gives:

This code did work earlier so I assume something has changed in setuptools - or is there something else missing ? Searching this project I see a `def reraise` in _setuptools/pkg_resources/_vendor/six.py_. The path made it seemed a bit "internal". What is the correct way to import this now ?

issue_title: Option for custom duplicate key handling
issue_body:
This is more a question than an issue: There is the `json` option, which allows for duplicate keys. It changes the regular behaviour to overwrite previously defined keys instead of throwing an error.

I would like to discuss/propose a change that would extend the `json` option – or maybe better yet introduce a new option – to define a function that allows for a custom handling of duplicate keys.
Use case: The `json` option overwrites keys completely and one would like to deep merge the existing structure with a partial one, which is the value of the duplicate key.

This logic could be implemented with a check for an already defined `_result[keyNode]` here:
https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml/blob/master/lib/js-yaml/loader.js#L309
It would then call the function provided via the option with `_result[keyNode]` (current value) and `valueNode` (new value, in the described use case the one that gets merged into the current value) and return the merged result.

My intent is to discuss the idea and possible implications of it, which I might not be aware of. Thanks! :)

issue_title: logging handler yields 'record' as string instead of map
issue_body:
When using the logging handler, the emitted record has type string (fixedstr) rather than map.

According to the fluent plugin docs at http://docs.fluentd.org/articles/plugin-development 'record' is required to be a Hash object, so I believe the implication is this module should only emit 'map'.

Example code (based on the examples from the docs), followed by some extra logging I added to fluentd's in_forward plugin:

Example using 'handler':

```
import logging
from fluent import handler

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
l = logging.getLogger('fluent.test')
l.addHandler(handler.FluentHandler('app.follow'))
l.info({
  'message': 'test from info',
})

2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: on_read_msgpack:
00000000  93 aa 61 70 70 2e 66 6f 6c 6c 6f 77 ce 55 28 4b  |..app.follow.U(K|
00000010  6b bd 7b 27 6d 65 73 73 61 67 65 27 3a 20 27 74  |k.{'message': 't|
00000020  65 73 74 20 66 72 6f 6d 20 69 6e 66 6f 27 7d     |est from info'}|
2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: on_message:  msg=["app.follow", 1428704107, "{'message': 'test from info'}"] chunk_size=47 source="host: 127.0.0.1, addr: 127.0.0.1, port: 49307"
2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: record_class=String
```

Example using 'event' directly, which works correctly:

```
from fluent import sender
from fluent import event
sender.setup('app')
event.Event('follow', {
  'message': 'this is a test',
})

2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: on_read_msgpack:
00000000  93 aa 61 70 70 2e 66 6f 6c 6c 6f 77 ce 55 28 4b  |..app.follow.U(K|
00000010  6b 81 a7 6d 65 73 73 61 67 65 ae 74 68 69 73 20  |k..message.this |
00000020  69 73 20 61 20 74 65 73 74                       |is a test|
2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: on_message:  msg=["app.follow", 1428704107, {"message"=>"this is a test"}] chunk_size=41 source="host: 127.0.0.1, addr: 127.0.0.1, port: 49306"
2015-04-10 22:15:07 +0000 [info]: record_class=Hash
```

Some output plugins do not care about the type of 'record' so this may appear to work, but there are other plugins that discard the record or have other issues if it is not a hash.

issue_title: imread automatically converts colour to grey image
issue_body:
## Description
When loading some colour images with `imread`, the image is converted to grey-level without requesting the conversion.

## Way to reproduce
Example image `example.png` (it is mostly grey but contains a green region on the bottom):
![example](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/8226248/31046051-d15c658a-a5e9-11e7-80a5-d6e92a4fcedf.png)

Code:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python
import skimage.io as io
import cv2

img = io.imread("example.png")
print "skimage",img.shape

cvimg = cv2.imread("example.png")
print "opencv",cvimg.shape
```

On my system I get:
```
skimage (240, 320)
opencv (240, 320, 3)
```
and the green colour (0,255,0) gets converted with skimage to grey 149.

I would expect from `io.imread` to return a 3-channel image, like it is provided by opencv's `imread`.

- scikit-image 0.13.1 (pip)
- Python 2.7.6
- Ubuntu 14.04

issue_title: segmentation.felzenszwalb document improvement
issue_body:
## Description
I tried to use felzenszwalb's segmentation algorithm in skimage, but some part of its doc is really confusing (at least for me).

Firstly, the `scale` parameter is described as `Free parameter. Higher means larger clusters.` but only after I read 
[the source code][1], I found it is exactly the constant `k` in the threshold function that described in the [original paper][2]. So I think the doc should add something like `the k used in threshold function`. By the way, I wonder why the default of `scale` is 1 but not a larger number that the author used like 150 or 300 which works better in common cases?

And secondly, I do not really understand that why `sigma` was described as `Width of Gaussian kernel used in preprocessing` especially the word `width`. But I think it is the `sigma` is standard deviation used in 2-D Gaussian function, isn't it?


[1]:https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/segmentation/_felzenszwalb_cy.pyx#L108
[2]:http://people.cs.uchicago.edu/~pff/papers/seg-ijcv.pdf

issue_title: Documentation no longer specifies HAE vs MSL for POS_LLH (0x0201)
issue_body:
In v1.0 of SBP.pdf, bit 3 of the flags in POS_LLH (0x0201) differentiated between WGS-84 (the height above a mathematically defined perfect ellipsoid that GPS uses internally) and height about the mean sea level (the height above a wobbly, bumby, ball of mass that smoothly follows the geoid).  I've seen the difference between the two systems vary between 5 to 30 meters.

In v2.0, this was removed from the documentation.  Bit 3 is labeled "Reserved".

It seems pretty important to convey which mode is being output.

issue_title: Help text for form fields
issue_body:
Bootstrap 4 uses a combination of `.form-text` and `.text-muted` to replace the old `.help-block` class for specifying help text on form elements. PR #49 makes this change in the django-bootstrap4 project code, but it keeps the help text in a `<div>` element, whereas the Bootstrap docs use a `<small>` instead (https://getbootstrap.com/docs/4.0/components/forms/#help-text), which renders smaller text.

The lack of a semantic classname (`.help-block` was great) makes it harder to isolate and target help text but it's still possible. `.form-group .form-text.text-muted {font-size: 80%}` works for me, but I wonder if it would be better to change the `field_help_text_and_errors.html` [template](https://github.com/zostera/django-bootstrap4/blob/master/bootstrap4/templates/bootstrap4/field_help_text_and_errors.html) to render a `<small>` instead of a `<div>`, so that a `{% bootstrap_field %}` template tag for a field with help text will match what is shown in the Bootstrap examples.

issue_title: RFC JSON Schema-based record editor (replacement for BibEdit)
issue_body:
Problem
-----------
`BibEdit` served well in Invenio `1.x` but is MARC-centric and was not designed for extensibility. As Invenio is moving towards using *jsonalchemy* as it's record representation (thus opening the ports for infinitely flexible record model), an hardcoded table-based web editor is no longer sufficient to natively edit Invenio records.

Proposal
-----------
Since *jsonalchemy* is about (inveniosoftware/jsonalchemy#9) to support [JSON Schemas](http://json-schema.org/) which is proposed standard to describe JSON models (including validation), we could automatically generate for the cataloger a form that allows him/her to edit a given record according to the schema. It happens that this is already implemented thanks to [json-editor](https://github.com/jdorn/json-editor). E.g. see: http://jeremydorn.com/json-editor/

Starting from this pre-existing editor one can build around/on top of it additional functionalities such as:
- [ ] record preview
- [ ] PDF preview
- [ ] auto-completion based on KBs
- [ ] automatic linking (e.g. when editing an arXiv ID, to be able to jump to it)
- [ ] server side validation based on JSONAlchemy
- [ ] keyboard shortcuts
- [ ] re-ordering through drag&drop
- [ ] editing references to other records (e.g. author signatures, affiliations, citations, superseeding... see #2573)
- [ ] shurtcuts such as *automatically generating new field instances by splitting the content of a given field

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/119349/6514194/cc4c9bbc-c380-11e4-88f1-262b6e85d168.png)

**Problem:**
In B2SHARE we would like to return the reason why a record was deleted when a client makes a GET request on a deleted request.
This is currently not possible as the [`PIDConverter`](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/d36b628c5687ca7342ce756bf42da53befb18eb7/invenio_records_rest/utils.py#L189) will [raise a `PIDDeletedRESTError` exception](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-rest/blob/d36b628c5687ca7342ce756bf42da53befb18eb7/invenio_records_rest/utils.py#L156).

I don't have yet any clear solution for this problem.
This is not an urgent issue.

issue_title: Rpc socket times out before EventHandler.waitAndGet
issue_body:
`json_rpc_base._SOCKET_TIMEOUT` is 60s. Yet `EventHandler.waitAndGet` can take an arbitrary timeout length.

`EventHandler.waitAndGet` makes a call to `EventSnippet.eventWaitAndGet` through the socket, which doesn't return until the event is received or the method itself times out.

So if somebody calls `EventHandler.waitAndGet` with a timeout longer than `json_rpc_base._SOCKET_TIMEOUT`, we'd get a socket timeout error.

We should:
1. Make socket timeout longer
2. Add a guard in `EventHandler.waitAndGet` for a max timeout value.

issue_title: Standby stuck in startup when WAL not available
issue_body: When a server comes back after a crash and master has recycled the necessary WAL the standby can get stuck in startup. It's not possible to prevent this with replication slots because Patroni doesn't have a persistent concept of cluster members, but that's a separate issue. When this happens PostgreSQL will sit in a startup loop trying and failing to fetch WAL from the master, Patroni will fail to do anything because connection to PostgreSQL fails. Reinit and restart on the API just time out. Only way out is to stop Patroni and rm -rf data directory. It would be nice if it was possible to use Patroni commands to resolve this kind of situation, or even better, automatically reinit the the standby when this happens.


If I comment out the ``syntax = "proto2";`` line the parsing progresses further. I am not sure if syntax is really an option but it appears that people are using it and the standard protobuf compiler does not have issues with it. It would be useful to to mirror the behaviour of the C++ protobuf compiler as close as possible.

issue_title: Random variate generator improvements
issue_body:
There are a few problems with the random variate generators.
1) They don't autobatch, although they should.
2) If you want random variates on the GPU, you have to create them on the CPU and copy them to the GPU.
3) The GaussianNoise function (which adds noise to an expression) also creates the random variates in a temporary buffer on the CPU, then copies it to the target device, then executes a copy. It also does not autobatch.
4) Dropout doesn't autobatch, it generates on the CPU and then copies the dropout mask (which has to live in auxiliary memory) to the appropriate device.

Fixing autobatching should be straightforward (although it is possible that the autobatching code will have to be adapted to deal correctly with artiy-0 functions, but this should be trivial). Dealing with random variate generation in a device-appropriate way will be more effort. First, the RNG state needs to be initialized in a sensible way on each device. Second, there may not be existing kernels for generating noise and adding it to a vector (needed only for GaussianNoise) or for generating all the random variate types DyNet offers. However, most of these kernels should be straightforward to implement.

issue_title: Glorot initialization is not good in VanillaLSTMBuilder
issue_body:
The parameters are created as two big `4*d_hid x d_hid`, `4*d_in x d_in` matrices so the implicit Glorot Initialization is done accordingly, whereas it should be done separately for each gate (and differently for tanh and sigmoid gates).

I don't know of any easy way to do this. Ideally one would create the four parameters separately and then stack them together but this doesn't seem easy to do with the current interface.

issue_title: Hello
issue_body:
Hello, I with help of my friend found an issue which is in when getting  a value from cache.
When two processes goes one by one, and function takes some time to return result,
both are checking the value in the cache, for example it is absent and both cache.get returns None, then both run function, which makes duplicated action.

So to avoid this behaviour you should use lock key and use cache.add function which returns True if value pushed in cache succesfully and false if it is already in the cache and <int> 0 if cache backend is not runing. cache.add is atomic function.

Here is my implementation of cached decorator
def cached(timeout, force_key=None):
    def _cached(func):
        func_type = _func_type(func)
        @wraps(func)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            if not hasattr(wrapper, '_full_name'):
                name, _args = _func_info(func, args)
                wrapper._full_name = name
            key_hash  = _cache_key(wrapper._full_name, func_type, args, kwargs, force_key)
            ok = cache.add(key_hash, True, timeout)  
            if ok is False:         # not ok is is not what is perfect it can be int 0  when backend not running
                start = time.time()
                while (time.time() - start) <= timeout and cache.get(key_hash):
                    result = cache.get(key_hash)
                    if isinstance(result, list):
                        return result[0]
                    else:
                        time.sleep(0.01)  wait fo result 
            elif ok ==0:
                pass
                # some action to inform user to start cache backend
            result = func(*args, **kwargs)
            cache.set(key_hash, [result], timeout)
            return result
        def invalidate(*args, **kwargs):
            if not hasattr(wrapper, '_full_name'):
                return
            key = _cache_key(wrapper._full_name, 'function', args, kwargs, force_key)
            cache.delete(key)
        wrapper.invalidate = invalidate
        return wrapper
    return _cached

issue_title: Subclassing tuple causes failure on de-serialization (but not serialization)
issue_body:
Simple code example:

```python
class Range(tuple):
    def __new__(cls, start, end):
        if start < 0 or end < 0:
            raise ValueError
        if start > end:
            raise ValueError("Range start is before end")
        return tuple.__new__(cls, (start, end))


new_range = Range(0,5)
a = dill.dumps(new_range)

#Exception happens here
dill.loads(a)
```


```
In [7]: dill.loads(a)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-7-46e4242c3d83> in <module>()
----> 1 dill.loads(a)

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/dill/dill.pyc in loads(str)
    271     """unpickle an object from a string"""
    272     file = StringIO(str)
--> 273     return load(file)
    274
    275 # def dumpzs(obj, protocol=None):

/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/dill/dill.pyc in load(file)
    261     pik = Unpickler(file)
    262     pik._main = _main_module
--> 263     obj = pik.load()
    264     if type(obj).__module__ == _main_module.__name__: # point obj class to main
    265         try: obj.__class__ == getattr(pik._main, type(obj).__name__)

/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.pyc in load(self)
    856             while 1:
    857                 key = read(1)
--> 858                 dispatch[key](self)
    859         except _Stop, stopinst:
    860             return stopinst.value

/usr/lib/python2.7/pickle.pyc in load_newobj(self)
   1081         args = self.stack.pop()
   1082         cls = self.stack[-1]
-> 1083         obj = cls.__new__(cls, *args)
   1084         self.stack[-1] = obj
   1085     dispatch[NEWOBJ] = load_newobj

TypeError: __new__() takes exactly 3 arguments (2 given)
```


I think this is because when subclassing an immutable object like tuple, and using the ```__new__``` function, the `*args` upon serialization get stored as the `tuple` accepts them, as a single tuple object, i.e `args = ((0,5),)` whereas upon deserialization, the class definition of our `Range` class expects it as `start, end`, i.e `args=(0,5,)`

One fix is by changing the definition of `__new__` method to accept `*args` and check `args[0]` for the type (whether or not it is a tuple) and go on to return the object based on parsing arguments, but was wondering if this was a bug or intended behaviour.

issue_title: GoBuildgen does not cull obsolete BUILDs
issue_body:
As reported by @dudeche:

In the `--materialize` mode, old BUILD files can be left around when packages move or are deleted.  This can lead to confusion, and worse, build errors when rglobbing targets with `::`.  In `--materialize` mode `GoBuildgen` should delete obsolete BUILD files.

This will require more care in the source-root checks `GoBuildgen` does.  It should be checking that not are its roots singular, but they are exclusive.  Currently there is no exclusivity check: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/tasks/go_buildgen.py#L248-L280

issue_title: Inject StageVariable into API resource definition that uses a Swagger file
issue_body:
When using a Swagger file to define an API, users have to explicitly specify `Stage Variables` to hold the Lambda function ARN and use the stage variable in their Swagger template to specify the integration. This is not intuitive.

We could simplify this process by automatically injecting the Stage Variable when users specify an API Event source to their Lambda Function

When customers specify a template like this:
```
LambdaFunction:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
  Properties:
      Handler: index.handler
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Events:
         GetApi:
             Type: API
              Properties:
                  Path: /
                  Method: GET
                  RestApiId: !Ref MyApiResource

MyApiResource:
   Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
   Properties:
        DefinitionUri: s3://bucket/myswagger.yaml
```

we could inject a StageVariable to the API resource where variable name is equal to the function resource's logical name and value equal to the function's ARN

```
MyApiResource:
   Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
   Properties:
        DefinitionUri: s3://bucket/myswagger.yaml
        Variables:
             LambdaFunction: !Ref LambdaFunction
```

issue_title: Use dockerd API version for integration tests
issue_body:
Some integration tests are wrapped in the `requires_api_version` decorator which purpose is to skip a test case if a client API version is lower than required one. In the latest version of docker-py, an instance of the test docker client defaults its version to 1.24 (via `DEFAULT_DOCKER_API_VERSION`), thus `requires_api_version` is always satisfied and it makes all test cases to fail if dockerd API version is lower than the client version.

I suggest to set the test docker client API version to dockerd API version. That would make `requires_api_version` to skip tests which require a newer API version and prevent from test cases failing with "client is newer than server".

issue_title: Add tests for Markov models with continuous factors
Potential error with current implementation of add_variables of NoisyOrModel
issue_body:
Suppose I have an NoisyOrModel instance of maximum cardinality (say 3)
```python
noisy = NoisyOrModel(['x1', 'x2', 'x3'], [2, 3, 2], [[0.6, 0.4],
....                                 [0.2, 0.4, 0.7], [0.1, 0.4]])
```
Internally we now have a 3x3 numpy array for inhibitor_probability
now,
```python
noisy.add_variables(['x4'],[4],[0.1,0.2,0.3,0.4])
```
would throw an error because of 
```python
self.inhibitor_probability = np.concatenate((self.inhibitor_probability, inhibitor_probability_uni))
```
I suggest that we keep track of the maximum cardinality to fix this

issue_title: Feature request. Change [-u USERNAME] to [-e EMAIL]
issue_body:
### Subject of the issue

Here is my situation. I was running:

```
coursera-dl -u LOGIN_I_ALWAYS_USE -p COURSERA_PASSWORD --debug COURSE_NAME
```

but got the error:

```
urllib3.connectionpool[_new_conn] Starting new HTTPS connection (1): www.coursera.org
urllib3.connectionpool[_make_request] https://www.coursera.org:443 "POST /api/login/v3 HTTP/1.1" 400 None
root[main] Could not authenticate: Cannot login on coursera.org: 400 Client Error: Bad Request for url: https://www.coursera.org/api/login/v3
```

It took me some time to find out that I should not use my standard login, but my email address. After I figure out the source of my problem I've remembered that this is the second time I'm doing the same mistake.

So, my feature request is to make this script simplier to use. Deprecate `-u` option and remove it from help and make `-e` option that is described as `[-e EMAIL] ` in help message.


### Your environment
* Operating System (name/version): docker image python:3.6.0
* Python version: 3.6.0
* coursera-dl version: 0.9.0

issue_title: Improve naming of ctypes_library and library
issue_body:
[ctypes_library.py](https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/blob/master/generated/nidmm/ctypes_library.py) contains a class called NidmmCtypesLibrary with 1 to 1 correspondence to the driver library functions.

[library.py](https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/blob/master/generated/nidmm/library.py) contains LibrarySingleton class and a couple of functions that *should* be private.

I propose:

* Rename NidmmCtypesLibrary to Library
    * Matches Session member name
    * Simpler (no codegen magic in class name)
* Rename library.py to library_singleton.py
* Rename  ctypes_library.py to library.py
* Move get_library_name() and get_library_type() to inside the class and pre-pend with _ (private)

I realized how we can reduce confusion on this while writing an overview of our testing strategy.

issue_title: Add some kind of `validate_config` command
issue_body:
It's pretty frustrating (especially to a new user) to have to go through data loading to figure out that you have an error in your model configuration.  And to fix that error and discover that there's another one, but it only told you one at a time.  It'd be a lot nicer to have some way to validate the configuration file before doing anything else.

It seems like @joelgrus's work on the configuration wizard should make this relatively straightforward.  We could add a `validate_config` command that just checked that your config was specified correctly, or, if it's fast enough, we could just always run that as the first thing in the `train` command, giving an error message with all of the problems, not just one at a time.

issue_title: Deis no longer drains app logs via `/deis/logs/drain`?

Looking to drain application logs to Papertrail. Docs (http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/platform_logging/#application-log-drain) suggest a method using `deisctl config logs set drain=...`, which apparently manages the `/deis/logs/drain` key in etcd. Is there another a new way to ship application logs?

issue_title: Test scripts should retry after Docker Hub errors
issue_body:
We often see transient 400 or 504 errors when pushing images to Docker Hub:
```
1af660919603: Pushing
time="2015-06-10T11:59:14-06:00" level="fatal" msg="HTTP code 400 while uploading metadata: {\"error\": \"This image does not belong to the repository\"}" 
```
or
```
f3e0d337c0f9: Pushing
time="2015-06-10T11:20:21-06:00" level="fatal" msg="HTTP code 504 while uploading metadata: unexpected end of JSON input" 
make[1]: *** [push] Error 1
```

Trying again generally succeeds without displaying the error. Ideally the `docker push` parts of our test scripts should catch these errors and simply retry the operation.

issue_title: Define a new option type which strictly requires that it is only passed once
issue_body:
We're currently using the Click default behavior when parsing options with `multiple=False`.
That parses all instances of an option and returns the last one given.

So `globus transfer ls --endpoint-id 'abc123' --endpoint-id 'def456'` is entirely valid, and results in `endpoint_id="def456"`.

This is fine/safe for options where allowing an override doesn't change the service-side semantics of commands, like `--format`, but for IDs and paths, it could be the result of user confusion and not result in the desired action.
For example, a `globus transfer async-transfer` with two sources, two destinations, and two paths is inherently ambiguous, and likely means that the user has wholly misunderstood usage of that command.

To correctly implement a change in this behavior, I think we need to add a new class of option.
Such opts can internally be specified with `multiple=True` and then assert that `len(value) <= 1` where `value` is the resulting tuple.
I'd suggest that this be a custom callback, but I worry about future opts that need or want a custom callback _and_ this behavior.
The custom option class doesn't add much more complexity, and properly handles that case.

issue_title: export toposort for conda-build
`pip install -e` does not recognise a conda environment as a virtualenv
issue_body:
I believe most people would agree that, for all intents and purposes, a conda environment should be treated like a proper Python virtualenv.

However, `pip install -e <git url>` (which installs a python package in "editable" mode) does not recognise a conda environment as such.

According to the documentation of pip, `pip install -e <git url>` would place the editable package under `<virtualenv name>/src` if pip detects a virtualenv; otherwise it places the editable package under the *current working directory*.

I first discovered the issue using berryconda (https://github.com/jjhelmus/berryconda/issues/12), which is a conda-based Python distribution for Raspberry Pi. This is what happens if I try to install a package in editable mode using berryconda:
```
$ cd
$ ls -ld ./src/django
ls: cannot access ./src/django: No such file or directory
```

This shows that we start off without a `~/src/django` directory.

Now let's create a conda environment and install django in editable mode:
```
$ conda create -n django-dev pip
Fetching package metadata .....
Solving package specifications: .

Package plan for installation in environment /home/pi/berryconda3/envs/django-dev:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    ca-certificates: 2017.7.27.1-0
    certifi:         2017.7.27.1-py36_0
    ncurses:         5.9-0
    openssl:         1.0.2l-0
    pip:             9.0.1-py36_0
    python:          3.6.2-0
    readline:        6.3-0
    setuptools:      36.2.7-py36_0
    sqlite:          3.15.0-0
    tk:              8.6.6-0
    wheel:           0.29.0-py36_0
    xz:              5.2.2-0
    zlib:            1.2.11-0

Proceed ([y]/n)? y

#
# To activate this environment, use:
# > source activate django-dev
#
# To deactivate an active environment, use:
# > source deactivate
#

$ source activate django-dev
$ pip install -e git+https://github.com/django/django.git#egg=django-dev
Obtaining django from git+https://github.com/django/django.git#egg=django-dev
  Cloning https://github.com/django/django.git to ./src/django
Collecting pytz (from django)
  Using cached pytz-2017.2-py2.py3-none-any.whl
Installing collected packages: pytz, django
  Running setup.py develop for django
Successfully installed django pytz-2017.2
$ ls -ld ./src/django
drwxr-xr-x 11 pi pi 4096 Aug 22 00:04 ./src/django
```

Oops.

Ok, so is there anything under `~/berryconda3/envs/django-dev/src`, where expect pip to have installed the editable package?
```
$ ls -ld ~/berryconda3/envs/django-dev/src
ls: cannot access /home/pi/berryconda3/envs/django-dev/src: No such file or directory
```

Crap.

I initially raised an issue at the berryconda repo (https://github.com/jjhelmus/berryconda/issues/12). However, @jjhelmus suggests that this is a problem upstream in conda or pip, so I'm raising a new issue here.

issue_title: Conda Perf on A-Cloud: reduce volume of repodata JSON being requested per operation
issue_body:
## Overview
When a user goes to perform a basic operation like install a conda package, the way it's asking for the information to perform the operation is hammering the server.... 

## Example:
* .condarc includes: anaconda, bioconda, r and conda-forge channels
* user goes to install python: `conda install python` (a very basic operation that will pull down python and 4 supporting files - see details below) 
* conda requests repo-data for every package, every file in all channels specified in .condarc
* Based on the way conda asks for the information, A-Cloud has to iterate over 5272 packages, and 100,000 files in those 5000 packages to gather the requested information. (for every anaconda install command - even if the request is fulfilled by repo.continuum.io
* conda's approach - fetch all the metadata for everything before calling teh solver to determine which source package to use for the installation



`conda create -n ENV_NAME python=2.7` results in....

`The following packages will be downloaded:`

    ---------------------------|-----------------
    vs2008_runtime-9.00.30729.1|                1         1.2 MB

issue_title: Snapshot recovery must work with wildcards
issue_body:
https://discuss.elastic.co/t/curator-throwing-error-while-restoring-failed-to-complete-action/89308/11

The issue is [here](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/v5.1.1/curator/utils.py#L1447):
```
    for index in index_list:
```

An index named for a wildcard will never be in the `response`, as it's not an actual index name.

The proposed solution will be:

```
   for index in response:
```

and to increase logging so that the provided indices and the found indices are both adequately described.  This should help when a wildcard does not make any matches.  A message that the restore completed may occur, but the logs will reveal why it said that, now.

issue_title: Documentation should point to elastic.co

issue_title: [VM/VMSS] Cannot create a new subnet on an existing Vnet
issue_body:
Scenario:  I created an app-gateway, which creates a VNET and a subnet. I want to add a VM to the backend pool, which requires the VM exists in the same VNET; however, the app-gateway itself has to be in its own subnet.  SO, what I'd like to do is create the VM, specify the existing VNET but specify a new subnet for that VNET. This does not work. The VM attempts to create a new VNET with the same name, which fails because the app-gateway is using that VNET.

VM create supports:
1. Specify nothing and use an existing VNET/subnet
2. Specify an existing VNET and subnet
3. Specify parameter to create a new VNET *and* subnet.

We should support a fourth scenario:
4. Specify parameter to create a new subnet on an existing VNET. 

The workaround is simply to create the extra subnet and then create the VM using the now-existing subnet.

---

### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)

The output looks like this:

```
import azureml
```

issue_title: Support for changed lines only
issue_body:
I've used [Overcommit](https://github.com/brigade/overcommit) in the past. Unfortunately, it is currently unable to run hooks that alter files. Pre-commit has been working great for this. However, there is one feature I miss - the ability to reference only the altered lines in a hook. 

For instance, you can compare the line number in an error message with the list of lines that are included in the diff, and then choose to ignore those unchanged lines. This can be very valuable in a legacy code base. 

Are there any provisions for such a thing in Pre-commit?

issue_title: _CoroGuard cannot be awaited
issue_body:
## Long story short

1) [_CoroGuard](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/helpers.py#L65) class has no implementation for `__await__` method, only `__iter__`. 
That's why`_CoroGuard` instance cannot be `awaited`, just `yielded from`.

2) Fix warning [message](https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/client.py#L538) for `ClientSession.close()` method

## Expected behaviour

- `_CoroGuard` can be awaited

## Actual behaviour

- `_CoroGuard` cannot be awaited

## Steps to reproduce

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
import aiohttp
import asyncio


async def coro_function(loop):
    session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=loop)
    await session.close()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    try:
        loop.run_until_complete(coro_function(loop))
    finally:
        loop.close()
```

## Your environment
```
OS X 10.12.3
python 3.6.0
aiohttp==2.2.0
```

issue_title: Request for app assistant update and Italian language
issue_body:
Hello to all,
for all those who live in Italy and can not view the devices registered in the app assistant, the problem is that you have to set up the phone in English and opening the app assistant and going into settings, magically appear the registered devices.
However, the Italian language for raspberry type custom devices is still not available.
It requires an update of the Italian assistant app that is different from the English one and the ability to set the Italian language.

issue_title: True read-only cells
issue_body:
nbgrader currently has pseudo "read-only" cells where they are "read-only" in the sense that their contents get saved to the nbgrader database, and then overwritten during grading if they have been changed by the student. I have reports that this is confusing, and requests for true read-only cells where the cell actually cannot be edited at all in the live notebook.

I think this would be really useful to have for nbgrader. We could keep it as an undocumented feature for the moment, just like "undeletable" cells are, until we can figure out an appropriate UI for it.

issue_title: Provide a log of unhandled output
issue_body: With widgets as outputs, they may have output that is unhandled and has no specific cell to be displayed. It would be great if there was some sort of log area where any unhandled output in the notebook could be stored (and rendered if the log is displayed) to aid in debugging.

issue_title: Allow gixy to accept multiple .config files at once
issue_body:
If you have a few different config files it would be nice to be able to run:

`gixy *.conf`

and have it check all config files. To allow this Gixy should be extended to accept any number of config files. It seems like it should be pretty simple to add support for this in the `cli` module.

issue_title: Observation space indexing doesn't match action space indexing
issue_body:
First, thank you very much for this awesome repository!!

--------
I have noticed that the observation-space indexing is different in observation-space (numpy) and in action-space. If I select an (x,y)-point from observations and want to move there I need to do (y,x) in the action-arguments.

Please consider the script below which is adapted from the MoveToBeacon -scripted agent.
The problem is explained after `####### SEE HERE:` -line.

```
import sys
import numpy as np
from absl import flags
from pysc2.env import sc2_env
from pysc2.lib import actions
from pysc2.lib.features import SCREEN_FEATURES

FLAGS = flags.FLAGS
flags.FLAGS(sys.argv)

res = 32
no_op = [actions.FunctionCall(0, [])]
select_all = [actions.FunctionCall(3, [[0], [0, 0], [res - 1, res - 1]])]
move_action_id = 331
player_neutral = 3

env = sc2_env.SC2Env(
    map_name="DefeatRoaches",
    step_mul=8,
    game_steps_per_episode=0,
    screen_size_px=(res, res),
    minimap_size_px=(res, res),
    visualize=True
)

timestep = env.reset()[0]

for i in range(500):
    player_rel = timestep.observation["screen"][SCREEN_FEATURES.player_relative.index]
    beacon_loc = player_rel == 3
    move_available = move_action_id in timestep.observation["available_actions"]
    beacon_visible = beacon_loc.any()
    if not (move_available and beacon_visible):
        timestep = env.step(select_all)

    x_beacon, y_beacon = [int(np.mean(c)) for c in np.where(beacon_loc)]
    ####### SEE HERE:
    # So this is where the beacon is in observations...
    assert player_rel[x_beacon, y_beacon] == 3
    # But to move there we need to flip the coordinates to (y, x) !!
    a = [actions.FunctionCall(move_action_id, [[0], [y_beacon, x_beacon]])]
    
    timestep = env.step(a)[0]

    a = [actions.FunctionCall(0, [])]
    timestep = env.step(a)[0]
    if timestep.last():
        print("Episode finished with score %d" %
              timestep.observation["score_cumulative"][0])

env.close()
``` 

I am Not sure if this is really a bug but I think it's worth opening a topic.  Maybe can save the frustration what I experienced debugging from someone else.

issue_title: connect to existing server
issue_body: I think it would be reasonable to allow connecting to an existing server. For example, the cli args could take a host/port to connect to, instead of starting a new server process. This is particularly useful if the sc2 server is running on a remote host

issue_title: monitor congestion on brokers with stats_cb/position/commited?
issue_body:
I have a consumer app and a producer app, and they will be exchanging possibly high-volume traffic over a kafka cluster. I don't get to put any of my own code on the cluster, it is just a generic kafka cluster. It would be interesting to me to see if the consumer is falling behind, and data was building up on the cluster from within my code (either producer or consumer). Is there a way to do this? On the consumer, I see that I can do consumer.position() and consumer.committed(), but their outputs don't look very interesting.

The stats_cb gives me a big blob of JSON, but I don't know how to interpret it. I can see each partition, and several different *_offset stats: hi_offset, next_offset, app_offset, eof_offset, and stored_offset. Sometimes these are all the same, some of them are -1001 (what does this mean?), and sometimes some are lower than others. When this is the case, is the difference an indication of how behind the consumer is? It seems that app_offset is always lower than or equal to hi_offset and next_offset, while eof_offset can be as high as hi_offset or lower than it.

Are the per-broker "throttle" stats what I want?

in general, is there a cheat sheet to help me interpret the stats_cb JSON blob? Barring that, is there a way to see what I want, that is, to determine if we are producing faster than we are consuming?

issue_title: End Explicit Support for Python 3.3
issue_body:
Python 3.3 has been EoL'ed since September, and more and more major packages are dropping support, making maintenance more difficult. Pytest, for example, [dropped support in version 3.3.0](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest/blob/master/CHANGELOG.rst#pytest-330-2017-11-23).

I think that unless there is a really good reason, we should stop explicitly supporting Python 3.3 going forward.

issue_title: Incompatibility  with datetutil.rrule and pytz.timezone?
issue_body:
For some time I've been using pytz's timezone implementation together with dateutil's rrule and have stumbled on an incompatibility several times. When expanding a RRULE over DST boundaries, those DST transition are disregarded (see below for an example). Is this a general incompatibility between those two packages, am I doing it wrong or is there a bug hidden somewhere (either in dateutil or pytz)?

```python
import pytz
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.rrule import rrulestr

BERLIN = pytz.timezone('Europe/Berlin')
local_time = BERLIN.localize(datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19))
date_list = list(rrulestr('FREQ=MONTHLY;COUNT=12', dtstart=local_time))
# translating these times to utc and back to berlin makes the error obvious
print([dtime.astimezone(pytz.UTC).astimezone(BERLIN) for dtime in date_list])

```
outputs:
```python
[datetime.datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CEST+2:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 5, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CEST+2:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 6, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CEST+2:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CEST+2:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 8, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CEST+2:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 9, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CEST+2:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CEST+2:00:00 DST>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 8, 18, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CET+1:00:00 STD>), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 8, 18, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CET+1:00:00 STD>), 
datetime.datetime(2016, 1, 8, 18, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CET+1:00:00 STD>), 
datetime.datetime(2016, 2, 8, 18, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CET+1:00:00 STD>), 
datetime.datetime(2016, 3, 8, 18, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Berlin' CET+1:00:00 STD>)]
```

doing the same with a dateutil timezone works fine:
```python
from datetime import datetime
from dateutil.rrule import rrulestr
from dateutil import tz

BERLIN = tz.gettz('Europe/Berlin')
local_time = datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19, tzinfo=BERLIN)
date_list = list(rrulestr('FREQ=MONTHLY;COUNT=12', dtstart=local_time))
print([dtime.astimezone(tz.tzutc()).astimezone(BERLIN) for dtime in date_list])
```
outputs:

```python
[datetime.datetime(2015, 4, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 5, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 6, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 8, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 9, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 10, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2015, 12, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2016, 1, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2016, 2, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin')), 
datetime.datetime(2016, 3, 8, 19, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('/usr/share/zoneinfo/Europe/Berlin'))]

```
